title: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
summary: Chapter 10 studies the structure and function of the human eye, common defects of vision and their correction, and the phenomena of atmospheric refraction including dispersion of light through a prism. The defects myopia, hypermetropia, and presbyopia are explained with their causes and corrections using lenses. The scattering of light explains why the sky is blue and the sun appears red at sunrise and sunset.
learningPoints: Describe the structure and function of the human eye: cornea, iris, pupil, lens, retina
Define power of accommodation of eye
Explain near point and far point of the eye
Define and explain myopia (near-sightedness): cause, symptoms, correction using concave lens
Define and explain hypermetropia (far-sightedness): cause, symptoms, correction using convex lens
Define presbyopia and its correction using bifocal lens
Explain dispersion of white light through prism — VIBGYOR order
Explain recombination of white light using an inverted prism
Explain atmospheric refraction: twinkling of stars, advance sunrise and delayed sunset
Explain Tyndall effect and scattering of light
Explain why sky is blue (Rayleigh scattering)
Explain why sun appears red during sunrise and sunset

laws.name: Tyndall Effect
laws.statement: When light passes through a colloid (fine particles), the particles scatter light. Blue light is scattered more than red. This is why sky appears blue and scattering is visible in smoke/dust.

laws.name: Rayleigh Scattering
laws.statement: Shorter wavelengths (blue, violet) are scattered more than longer wavelengths (red, orange). Sky appears blue because scattered blue light reaches our eyes from all directions.

formulas.label: Power of Accommodation
formulas.description: Ability of eye lens to adjust focal length. Normal eye: near point 25cm, far point infinity.

formulas.label: Order of Spectrum (VIBGYOR)
formulas.expression: Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red
formulas.description: Violet has shortest wavelength (most deviated). Red has longest wavelength (least deviated) in prism.

diagrams.title: Structure of Human Eye
diagrams.description: Cornea (outer transparent), Iris (coloured, controls pupil size), Pupil (opening for light), Crystalline lens (adjustable, controlled by ciliary muscles), Retina (image formed), Optic nerve (signals to brain), Blind spot (no photoreceptors).

diagrams.title: Correction of Myopia
diagrams.description: Myopic eye: image formed in front of retina. Correction: concave lens diverges light rays so they focus on retina.

diagrams.title: Dispersion through Prism
diagrams.description: White light enters prism → splits into VIBGYOR spectrum. Violet deviates most, red least. Emerged as coloured band on screen.

resources.label: Karnataka SSLC Science Textbook
resources.description: Official Karnataka SSLC Science textbook Chapter 10 Human Eye.

resources.label: NCERT Science Textbook Class 10
resources.description: NCERT Class 10 Science Chapter 11 The Human Eye.

resources.label: Human Eye — Video Lessons
resources.description: Khan Academy videos on eye structure, vision defects, and light scattering.

pyqs.question: Mention any two symptoms of myopic eye. How can this defect be rectified? What is power of accommodation?
pyqs.answer: Myopia symptoms: (i) High curvature of eye lens (ii) Eyeball longer than usual. Correction: concave lens. Power of accommodation: ability of eye lens to adjust its focal length to see near and distant objects clearly.

pyqs.question: What is near-sightedness? How does eye accommodate to see distant objects?
pyqs.answer: Myopia = near-sightedness. Can see nearby clearly, not distant. For distant objects: ciliary muscles relax, lens becomes thin → focal length increases → image on retina.

pyqs.question: Why do stars twinkle but planets do not?
pyqs.answer: Stars twinkle: light from distant stars refracts continuously in atmosphere (different refractive index layers) making the position of star appear to shift rapidly — twinkling effect. Planets are closer, appear as extended source — refraction effects average out — no twinkling.

mcqs.question: Symptoms of myopic eye include:
options: B. High curvature of lens and longer eyeball
mcqs.explanation: Myopia causes: lens too curved OR eyeball too long → image forms in front of retina. Cannot see distant objects.

mcqs.question: Myopia is corrected by:
mcqs.explanation: Concave lens diverges light so it converges on retina (rather than in front). Hypermetropia corrected by convex lens.

mcqs.question: Power of accommodation of eye is:
options: A. Ability to see only distant objects
B. Ability to adjust focal length to see near and distant objects
C. Maximum focal length of lens
D. Minimum focal length of lens
mcqs.explanation: Power of accommodation = ability of ciliary muscles to change lens curvature to adjust focal length for near and far vision.

mcqs.question: Sky appears blue because:
options: A. Sky is blue in colour
B. Blue light is more scattered by atmosphere
C. Sun emits blue light preferentially
mcqs.explanation: Rayleigh scattering: shorter wavelengths (blue, violet) scatter more by air molecules. Scattered blue light reaches us from all directions making sky appear blue.

mcqs.question: Sun appears red/orange during sunrise and sunset because:
options: A. Sun emits more red light
B. Blue light is scattered away; only red/orange reaches us
mcqs.explanation: At sunrise/sunset, sunlight travels longer path through atmosphere. Blue light scattered away. Only longer wavelength red/orange reaches eyes directly.

mcqs.question: Order of colours in spectrum from violet to red represents:
mcqs.explanation: VIBGYOR: Violet (shortest λ, most deviated) to Red (longest λ, least deviated). Wavelength increases V→R.

mcqs.question: Stars twinkle because:
options: B. Continuous refraction in atmospheric layers
C. Stars are very far away
mcqs.explanation: Different layers of atmosphere have different refractive indices and temperatures. Starlight continuously refracts → apparent position shifts rapidly → twinkling.

mcqs.question: The near point of a normal human eye is:
mcqs.explanation: Normal near point = 25 cm (distinct vision distance). Objects closer than 25 cm cannot be seen clearly by normal eye.

mcqs.question: Presbyopia occurs due to:
options: B. Loss of power of accommodation with age
mcqs.explanation: With age, ciliary muscles weaken and lens loses flexibility → reduced power of accommodation. Cannot see near or far clearly. Corrected by bifocal lenses.

mcqs.question: White light recombines when:
options: A. Passed through another prism in same orientation
B. Passed through an inverted prism
mcqs.explanation: Newton's experiment: second inverted prism recombines VIBGYOR spectrum back into white light, proving white light is a mixture of seven colours.

mcqs.question: Tyndall effect explains:
options: B. Scattering of light by colloidal particles
mcqs.explanation: Tyndall effect: colloidal particles scatter light making the beam visible. Examples: sunlight through trees, headlights in fog, milk appearing white.

mcqs.question: Cataract is:
options: B. Opaque crystalline lens blocking vision
mcqs.explanation: Cataract = clouding of crystalline lens, blocking light. Treatment: surgical removal of cloudy lens and replacement with artificial lens.

mcqs.question: During eye accommodation for near objects:
options: A. Ciliary muscles relax, lens flattens
B. Ciliary muscles contract, lens becomes more curved
mcqs.explanation: For near objects: ciliary muscles contract → suspensory ligaments loosen → lens bulges (more curved) → shorter focal length → image on retina.

mcqs.question: Why do clouds appear white?
options: B. Clouds scatter all wavelengths equally (larger particles)
D. Clouds are white in colour
mcqs.explanation: Cloud droplets are much larger than air molecules. They scatter all visible wavelengths (colours) equally → white appearance.

mcqs.question: Hypermetropia is caused by:
options: C. Too short eyeball or less curved lens
mcqs.explanation: Hypermetropia: eyeball too short or lens too flat → image forms behind retina → cannot see near objects clearly. Corrected by convex lens.

exercises.question: Explain myopia with diagram. How is it corrected?
exercises.answer: Myopia (near-sightedness): image forms in front of retina because eyeball too long or lens too curved. Causes: excessive curvature, genetic, excessive near work. Symptoms: cannot see distant objects clearly. Correction: concave (diverging) lens diverges light so it converges on retina.

exercises.question: Why do stars twinkle but planets do not?
exercises.answer: Stars are extremely far away (point sources). Light refracts through different atmospheric layers (varying temperature/density/refractive index) causing apparent position to shift rapidly — twinkling. Planets are closer — appear as extended disc source. Average refraction over disc prevents twinkling.

exercises.question: Explain dispersion of white light through a glass prism.
exercises.answer: White light (mixture of 7 colours) enters prism. Different wavelengths have different refractive indices → different deviations. Violet (shortest λ) deviates most; Red (longest λ) deviates least. Result: VIBGYOR spectrum. This proves white light is mixture of colours.

exercises.question: Describe the structure of human eye and function of each part.
exercises.answer: Cornea: transparent, first refraction. Iris: coloured, controls pupil size. Pupil: opening, regulates light. Crystalline lens: adjustable converging, focuses light. Ciliary muscles: adjust lens curvature. Retina: screen, contains rods (dim light) and cones (colour, bright light). Optic nerve: signals to brain.

exercises.question: Explain the Tyndall effect with examples.
exercises.answer: Tyndall effect: fine particles in colloid scatter light, making beam visible. Examples: (i) Sunbeams visible through forest — dust particles scatter sunlight (ii) Headlights in fog — fog particles scatter light (iii) Milk appears white — fat globules scatter light (iv) Blue sky — air molecules scatter blue light.

exercises.question: Why does sun appear red/orange at sunrise and sunset?
exercises.answer: At sunrise/sunset, sunlight travels through maximum atmosphere thickness (longer path). Blue (short λ) is scattered away in all directions. Mostly red and orange wavelengths (long λ) travel straight to reach our eyes directly — sun appears red/orange.

exercises.question: What is power of accommodation? What happens to it with age?
exercises.answer: Power of accommodation = ability of ciliary muscles to change lens curvature to adjust focal length for near and far vision. Young eye: high power of accommodation (near point ~25cm, far point infinity). With age: ciliary muscles weaken, lens becomes less flexible → presbyopia — requires bifocal lenses.

exercises.question: Distinguish between myopia, hypermetropia and presbyopia.
exercises.answer: Myopia: cannot see far, image in front of retina, corrected by concave lens. Hypermetropia: cannot see near, image behind retina, corrected by convex lens. Presbyopia: old age, cannot see both near and far, loss of accommodation, corrected by bifocal lens (concave+convex).
